news_summary: Congress is advancing reforms to streamline disaster recovery and accelerate the construction of new homes.
news_summary_fr: Le Congrès fait avancer des réformes visant à rationaliser les opérations de reconstruction après une catastrophe et à accélérer la construction de nouveaux logements.
news_summary_es: El Congreso está impulsando reformas para agilizar la recuperación tras las catástrofes y acelerar la construcción de nuevas viviendas.

articles.title: Congress set to overhaul disaster recovery, speeding up new home builds
articles.content:
There are a lot of ways for disaster victims to get help in the immediate aftermath of a flood or fire. The Federal Emergency Management Agency gives out temporary housing assistance and pays for debris removal, insurance companies pay to rebuild homes and replace lost valuables, and charity groups pass out food and water.

But the long-term recovery from a major disaster is a lot harder. It can take a community years to build back from a major hurricane or wildfire, and the United States only has one federal program to help. Run by the Department of Housing and Urban Development, or HUD, the Community Development Block Grant Disaster Recovery program has provided more than $100 billion to disaster areas over the last few decades, but almost everyone agrees that it is broken. The program operates on an ad hoc basis, without permanent congressional approval, so most of its disaster grants take more than half a decade to execute and arrive too late to make a difference.

That is about to change. Last month, Congress passed a major bipartisan housing bill with an endorsement from President Donald Trump. The president has said he will not sign the bill because Congress has not passed voting restrictions, but it will become law at the end of today if he does not veto it. Congress has enough votes to override a potential veto anyway.

The bill’s primary focus is on building more homes in areas that are suffering from housing shortages, but it also contains a little-noticed provision that will turn HUD’s sporadic disaster aid into a permanent program. The change could prevent the stagnation that often happens in disaster areas when displaced families struggle to find housing and businesses shut down.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

“All the tumblers are there to make it work now,” said Stan Gimont, a former HUD official who oversaw the disaster grant program under the Obama and first Trump administrations. “They all line up, and it should all go a lot more quickly.”

The issues with HUD’s block grant recovery program are numerous and notorious. Without permanent authorization from Congress, lawmakers have to approve new investments after each disaster, which can take months or years if it happens at all. Similarly, the department has to go through an onerous federal rulemaking process every time it receives money. Even once a disaster-affected community gets approval to spend money on new housing, or tenant assistance, or rebuilt infrastructure, it can take years to conduct the necessary environmental reviews.

A case in point is the coastal town of New Bern, North Carolina, which lost hundreds of affordable apartments after it was flooded by Hurricane Florence in 2018. A Grist investigation of disaster recovery there found that it took more than five years to build a few dozen apartments with HUD money. By the time the new complexes opened in New Bern, the tenants who had lost their apartments back in 2018 had moved on to other cities.

In other cases, the spending timelines have extended even longer. Some grantees who received money after the 2017 California wildfires or 2012’s Hurricane Sandy are still trying to access the funds owed to them. In a report last month, HUD said it was “targeting for closeout” a grant that was supposed to help Arkansas recover from tornadoes that occurred in 2008. A 2024 report by the department’s internal auditor found that these delays were caused by the program’s ad hoc authorization and by failed coordination between HUD and other agencies.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

“Without permanent authorization and a permanent funding stream, you’re kind of left reinventing the wheel every time,” said Stan Gimont, the former HUD official, who is now a senior advisor at the disaster recovery consulting firm Hagerty Consulting.

The language in the new housing bill would free up HUD to spend money without waiting for congressional approval, endow a new disaster recovery fund, and create a permanent disaster unit within the department. This is similar to how FEMA, housed within the Department of Homeland Security, operates now. An accelerated timeline would allow HUD to start spending money in disaster areas as soon as FEMA’s aid winds down, rather than having to wait years after a disaster strikes. This could speed up the construction of new affordable apartment buildings or the demolition of flood-prone homes, or even fast-track financial support for new businesses and job training programs.

“It really helps,” said Carlos Martín, the vice president for research at the think tank Resources for the Future, who has spent years studying the HUD program. “FEMA assistance goes for 18 months maximum, and then HUD assistance often wouldn’t kick in for two or three years after that. There’s been this valley of death in recovery, and this just decreases that valley of death to being more like a crack, and fewer people can slip through that crack.”

The new authorization in the housing bill is not technically permanent; in order to appease Republicans in the House of Representatives, lawmakers added a sunset provision that will wind down the aid program after three years. But another “sense of Congress” clause in the law suggests that the block-grant program could continue indefinitely as long as there is money available, and Martín said it’s unlikely lawmakers will wind it down after creating a new unit to manage it.

“It really was a bipartisan agreement,” he said.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant bipartisan legislative change that will transform the HUD disaster recovery program from an ad hoc, slow process into a permanent, streamlined system. This change promises faster, more effective long-term recovery for disaster-affected communities across the U.S., addressing a major gap in housing and infrastructure rebuilding. The impact is broad and meaningful for many people living in disaster-prone areas, with detailed context on the program's past failures and the improvements expected.
articles.personality_title: Congress reforms disaster recovery to speed up rebuilding homes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – After natural disasters like floods and wildfires, people often get quick help such as temporary housing and food. But rebuilding homes and communities takes much longer. The U.S. has one main program run by the Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) to help with long-term recovery, but it has been slow and unreliable.

**What happened** – Congress recently passed a housing bill that includes a new plan to fix HUD’s disaster recovery program. Instead of waiting for Congress to approve funds after each disaster, HUD will have a permanent disaster fund and a special disaster recovery team. This will let HUD act faster to rebuild homes and support communities.

**Impact** – This change is important because the old program often took years to help people. For example, after Hurricane Florence in 2018, it took over five years to rebuild apartments in New Bern, North Carolina. Now, with faster funding and fewer delays, families can return to their homes sooner and communities can recover more quickly.

**What's next step** – The bill will become law soon, even if the president vetoes it, because Congress can override the veto. The new program will start working right after FEMA’s short-term aid ends in disaster areas. Although the program is set to last three years, experts believe it will continue beyond that because it has strong support.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Congress has created a faster, permanent system to help communities rebuild homes and recover after disasters, ending years of delay in disaster aid.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Congrès réforme la récupération après sinistre pour accélérer la reconstruction des logements
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Après des catastrophes naturelles comme les inondations et les incendies, les victimes reçoivent souvent une aide rapide comme un logement temporaire et de la nourriture. Mais reconstruire les maisons et les communautés prend beaucoup plus de temps. Les États-Unis ont un programme principal géré par le Département du logement (HUD) pour la récupération à long terme, mais il était lent et peu fiable.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le Congrès a récemment adopté une loi sur le logement qui inclut un nouveau plan pour réparer le programme de récupération après sinistre de HUD. Au lieu d’attendre l’approbation du Congrès après chaque catastrophe, HUD aura un fonds permanent et une équipe spéciale de récupération. Cela permettra à HUD d’agir plus rapidement pour reconstruire les maisons et soutenir les communautés.

**Impact** – Ce changement est important car l’ancien programme prenait souvent des années pour aider les gens. Par exemple, après l’ouragan Florence en 2018, il a fallu plus de cinq ans pour reconstruire les appartements à New Bern, en Caroline du Nord. Désormais, avec un financement plus rapide et moins de retards, les familles peuvent revenir chez elles plus tôt et les communautés se rétablir plus vite.

**Prochaine étape** – La loi deviendra bientôt effective, même si le président la met son veto, car le Congrès peut passer outre. Le nouveau programme commencera à fonctionner juste après la fin de l’aide à court terme de la FEMA dans les zones sinistrées. Bien que le programme soit prévu pour trois ans, les experts pensent qu’il continuera au-delà grâce à son fort soutien.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Le Congrès a créé un système permanent et plus rapide pour aider les communautés à reconstruire leurs maisons et à se remettre des catastrophes, mettant fin à des années de retard dans l’aide aux sinistrés.
articles.personality_title_es: El Congreso reforma la recuperación tras desastres para acelerar la reconstrucción de viviendas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Después de desastres naturales como inundaciones e incendios, las personas suelen recibir ayuda rápida como viviendas temporales y comida. Pero reconstruir casas y comunidades lleva mucho más tiempo. Estados Unidos tiene un programa principal dirigido por el Departamento de Vivienda (HUD) para la recuperación a largo plazo, pero ha sido lento y poco confiable.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, el Congreso aprobó una ley de vivienda que incluye un nuevo plan para arreglar el programa de recuperación tras desastres de HUD. En lugar de esperar la aprobación del Congreso después de cada desastre, HUD tendrá un fondo permanente y un equipo especial de recuperación. Esto permitirá que HUD actúe más rápido para reconstruir casas y apoyar a las comunidades.

**Impacto** – Este cambio es importante porque el programa antiguo a menudo tardaba años en ayudar a las personas. Por ejemplo, después del huracán Florence en 2018, tomó más de cinco años reconstruir apartamentos en New Bern, Carolina del Norte. Ahora, con fondos más rápidos y menos retrasos, las familias pueden volver a sus hogares antes y las comunidades pueden recuperarse más rápidamente.

**Próximo paso** – La ley se convertirá en ley pronto, incluso si el presidente la veta, porque el Congreso puede anular el veto. El nuevo programa comenzará a funcionar justo después de que termine la ayuda a corto plazo de FEMA en las áreas afectadas. Aunque el programa está previsto para tres años, los expertos creen que continuará más allá debido al fuerte apoyo.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El Congreso ha creado un sistema permanente y más rápido para ayudar a las comunidades a reconstruir viviendas y recuperarse tras desastres, terminando con años de demora en la ayuda.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a sturdy, newly built cluster of simple apartment buildings rising steadily on a sunlit hillside beside a calm river, with symbolic construction cranes gently lifting building blocks shaped like puzzle pieces into place, while a winding path connects the homes to a distant town skyline under a soft pastel sky, evoking hope, renewal, and accelerated recovery after disaster.
